body: A standalone birth centre is a midwifery-led unit separate from a hospital obstetric unit. It provides care for low-risk women during labour and birth, typically with no on-site obstetric, neonatal, or anaesthetic services. Evidence from the Birthplace UK cohort study shows comparable neonatal outcomes to obstetric units for low-risk women, with significantly fewer interventions.
title: What is a standalone birth centre?

body: Community midwifery in rural Ireland faces significant structural challenges: dispersed caseloads, long travel times, limited backup, and inadequate postnatal staffing. In HSE West, community midwives cover large geographic areas across Connemara, south Galway, and the islands, often as sole practitioners. Research from NUIG and HSE West highlights that day-5 postnatal visit coverage is particularly affected by these geographic constraints.
title: Community midwifery in rural Ireland

body: Antenatal care in Ireland is shared between GPs, obstetricians, and midwives. Community midwives in HSE West provide antenatal visits in health centres and home settings, covering topics including fetal development, birth planning, breastfeeding preparation, and mental health screening. The NICE antenatal care guidelines and HSE National Maternity Strategy (2016-2026) both inform current practice.
title: Antenatal care pathways in HSE West

body: The Galway Birth Centre Campaign was established in 2023 by local midwives, families, and community advocates to lobby for a standalone freestanding midwifery unit in Galway city. The campaign has submitted evidence to HSE West, engaged with local TDs and senators, and organised public consultations including a standing-room-only event at Galway City Hall in October 2025.
title: About the Galway Birth Centre Campaign

body: The Birthplace UK study (2011) followed over 64,000 women giving birth in England and found that for low-risk women, freestanding midwifery units were as safe as obstetric units in terms of neonatal outcomes. Women in freestanding units had significantly lower rates of interventions, including caesarean section, instrumental delivery, and epidural use. This evidence is frequently cited in Irish birth centre advocacy.
title: Birthplace UK study: key findings for Irish advocates

body: The 2016 Cochrane review on midwife-led continuity models of care found that women receiving care from a known midwife throughout pregnancy and birth were less likely to experience preterm birth, regional analgesia, episiotomy, or instrumental birth. They were also more likely to be satisfied with care. The review included 15 trials and over 17,000 women.
title: Cochrane review on midwife-led continuity of care

body: Homebirth services in Ireland are provided by the HSE Community Midwifery Service and a small number of self-employed independent midwives. Women must meet HSE criteria for low-risk status. In HSE West, community midwives support planned homebirth within their catchment areas, though geographic constraints in Connemara can limit access for some families.
title: Homebirth services in HSE West

body: The day-5 postnatal visit is a mandatory community midwifery contact that typically includes newborn weight check, feeding assessment, maternal emotional wellbeing screening, and neonatal bloodspot follow-up. In rural areas of HSE West, completing this visit within the 72-to-120-hour window presents logistical challenges due to caseload geography and staffing levels.
title: Day-5 postnatal visit: clinical and logistical overview

body: Neonatal resuscitation training (NRP) is mandatory for all community midwives in Ireland. Recertification is required every two years. The 2023 update to the NRP algorithm introduced changes to initial assessment steps and positive pressure ventilation guidance. Community midwives in solo practice must maintain NRP competence independently, often without access to hospital simulation facilities.
title: NRP recertification for community midwives

body: The HSE National Maternity Strategy 2016-2026 outlines a vision for woman-centred, safe, and accessible maternity services in Ireland. It recommends expanding community midwifery, developing midwifery-led units, and improving continuity of care. Freestanding birth centres are explicitly referenced as a model to explore, though implementation has been slow in the west.
title: HSE National Maternity Strategy 2016-2026

body: Galway University Hospital is the main maternity unit for the west of Ireland, serving over 3,000 births per year. It operates as a consultant-led obstetric unit with a co-located midwifery-led unit (MLU). Campaign advocates argue that a freestanding midwifery unit separate from the hospital site would increase choice and reduce unnecessary medicalisation for low-risk women.
title: Galway University Hospital maternity services overview

body: Families considering their birth options in Galway can choose between: consultant-led care at Galway University Hospital, midwifery-led care within the hospital MLU (if eligible), home birth with the HSE community midwifery service (if within catchment and low-risk), or private obstetric care. A freestanding birth centre would add a further safe option for eligible low-risk women.
title: Birth choices for families in Galway

body: Breastfeeding support in community settings is provided by community midwives, public health nurses, and lactation consultants. HSE West breastfeeding rates at discharge are below the national average. Community midwives play a key role in early breastfeeding establishment, particularly during the first week postnatal. A birth centre model would allow more continuity between birth and early postnatal breastfeeding support.
title: Breastfeeding support in HSE West community settings

body: Perinatal mental health is an increasing priority for Irish maternity services. Community midwives use validated screening tools including the Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) at routine antenatal and postnatal visits. Referral pathways to perinatal psychiatry and psychology are limited in HSE West, particularly for women in rural areas of Connemara.
title: Perinatal mental health screening in community midwifery

body: Frequently asked questions about the Galway Birth Centre Campaign: Who is eligible to use a birth centre? Low-risk women at term. Will it be free? Yes, as an HSE service. When will it open? The campaign is still at the feasibility and advocacy stage — no opening date has been set. How can I support it? Sign the petition, attend consultations, and contact your local TD.
title: Galway Birth Centre Campaign — Frequently Asked Questions
